clean_story:
Storm speed bumps. Nature’s flood brakes.

The phrase "soften storms" fits because forests act like a giant, living sponge—absorbing, slowing, and scattering what would otherwise hit hard and fast.

A storm’s rage moves in straight lines: wind howls unchecked, rain hammers the ground like a drum. But a forest turns chaos into order. Trees intercept raindrops midair, their leaves breaking falls into mist—like catching eggs with a bedsheet instead of concrete. Roots, tangled deep, turn soil into a water vault, storing 50% more rain than open fields. When wind slams into trunks, it splinters into weaker gusts, losing speed as if hitting a wall of feathers. Studies show forests can cut floodwater volume by 20% and reduce landslide risk by 64%.

Mangroves do this best. Their arching roots trap sediment, building natural levees that rise with the tides. During Typhoon Haiyan, villages behind intact mangroves saw waves 30% shorter than those without. Even dead trees help: fallen logs act like dams, forcing water to seep slowly into the earth instead of rushing downstream.

Three related subjects are coastal root systems, urban heat islands, and how beavers engineer wetlands.
